title: How I 10x'd My Cursor Coding Game—and Stopped Yelling at My AI Sidekick Forever
author: Akos Komuves
text:
Ask Mode - End AI Writing Code for No Reason - Real-Life Prompt Sequence Included
Hi friends,
This is Akos, and I’m the author of Model Context Protocol: Build MCP Servers with TypeScript, and I’m happy to announce the paperback version! 📖 But make sure to check out my author page as well because my books are bought by web developers, photographers, and all around the world, from the USA to Japan. They are easy to follow, contain no extra fluff, only the necessary stuff. 👏 So, last week,
My coding experience in Cursor just got a 10x upgrade.
The solutions I get are more like how I’d write the code.
I do only minor refactorings, and most importantly, yell less at Cursor.
Yes, we’re talking about the same Cursor.
And yes, you can have the same upgrade.
Let me explain.
Oh, if you haven’t subscribed yet, make sure you click here so you’ll get real, unfiltered, unsponsored insights like this one:
In Cursor, you can change from three modes in which you want to operate the agent:
Agent - the IDE edits the files to accomplish the task you asked for
Ask - operates the IDE in read-only mode, only searches the codebase
Plan - comes up with a detailed plan to achieve your goal
You’ll find these modes in other editors (Windsurf, VSCode Copilot), named a bit differently or you can just ask the Agent to not edit, only answer.
Here’s the caveat that 99% developers fall into, and then complain about Agentic coding being trash, and we are not there yet, and so on:
When you operate the IDE in Agent mode, it strives to code.
Built to Code
It’s like a junior developer who’s eager to show you, “I’ve got this”.
Perhaps the solution is already there, or will be there if you remove unnecessary code, but no no.
Agent was designed to write code, so it’ll code.
Give it the wrong context and it’ll rebuild your backend from scratch.
Does this reply sound familiar?
Ah, I see now! I made a mistake by not sending the ID to the backend.
I can’t find the resource you’re mentioning, so let me create it. *wants to create backend endpoint 🤦♂️*
I can’t find the *picks random technology* Express router configuration, so let me create it. *initializes Express in a Next.js project 🤦♂️*
I can’t find the Prisma configuration, so let me set up Prisma. *when you’ve been using Drizzle the whole time 🤦♂️*
You must have seen this if you’re using agentic coding and you thought: This is trash.
But what if you are using it wrong?
Context
One thing we can be sure about:
The bottleneck with agentic coding, or really, anything that involves LLMs generating something, is usually not on the output side.
They write you a poem about floppy disks just as easily as a 10,000-word essay about why TypeScript is better than Python.
It is in the input, where you can influence what you’ll be getting.
Tell the agent you’re using Next.js, and it’ll automatically look for the right configuration files.
It’s all about giving as much context as possible to the agent, so when it comes to execution, it knows exactly where you are at right now.
But this is how we humans function, right?
Ask
In a product meeting where they throw a bunch of visionary ideas at you, you’ll unlikely reply with a hell yeah!
Instead, you ask some follow-up questions:
How should we handle new accounts vs existing ones?
Do we need SSO?
Where will this be hosted?
Do we need an option for self-hosting?
Well, recently, that’s pretty much how I spend my time in Cursor.
I’ve taken this screenshot, using most of my context window, but the interesting part is that I’ve only used Ask up until this point.
In Action
Here are my prompts from a recent discussion with Cursor.
I had to implement a cleanup mechanism after a failed newsletter generation. The newsletter sections are generated using several independent jobs, so I couldn’t take the transaction route, where I simply don’t commit the transaction when an error occurs.
This made things a little more complex, but after asking the right questions, Cursor exactly knew what to do.
For clarity, I have only included a summary of what Cursor replies with, but I have left my prompts unedited.
Me: can you identify what we do when generating a newsletter fails?
Cursor: Explained the failure handling mechanism for newsletter generation. When the main job fails, it logs errors, marks progress as ‘error’ via `errorProgress()`, and captures exceptions to Sentry. For subscriber generation, if ≥20% of variables fail, the job stops with a `NonRetriableError`. The system tracks partial failures and stops if the threshold is exceeded.
---
Me: okay, which one of these create db entries and where?
Cursor: Clarified that all failure handling updates the existing `newsletters` row via the `metadata` JSONB field - no new rows are created. The `errorProgress()` method updates `newsletters.metadata.subscriberProgress.status` to `’error’` and adds log entries. The `updateSubscriberVariables()` method tracks failed variable counts.
---
Me: what is the first step in the newsletter generation?
Cursor: Identified that the first step is `startProgress()` which updates `newsletters.metadata` to mark progress as started. The first database entry creation is `createNEmptySubscribers()` which inserts empty subscriber records into the `subscribers` table (after deleting any existing subscribers).
---
Me: okay, I want to focus on the step that’s causing the UI to display “Creating Newsletter Failed”
Cursor: Explained that the UI shows “Creating Newsletter Failed” when `newsletters.metadata.subscriberProgress.status` is set to `’error’`. This happens when `errorProgress()` is called, which occurs in two places: the `onFailure` handler (when the job fails after all retries) and the main catch block (when an unhandled exception occurs).
---
Me: can you tell me where db entries are being generated when this job runs?
Cursor: Provided a comprehensive breakdown of all database INSERT operations during the `generateSubscribers` job:
| Table | Count | When |
|-------|-------|------|
| `subscribers` | N (newsletter.subscribersCount) | Initial step (line 211) |
| `audience_clusters` | 1 | After RAG (line 289) |
| `audience_clusters_to_newsletters` | 1 | After RAG (line 289) |
| `usage` | 1 (or gets existing) | Multiple times during generation |
| `usage_item` | Many (one per LLM call) | Throughout generation |
| `newsletter_chat` | N (one per subscriber) | When newsletters start (line 455) |
| `newsletter_messages` | Many (2+ per question per subscriber) | During newsletter execution |
---
Me: would it be possible to somehow keep a reference to what was created when the error occurred and clean up the entries?
And if possible, keep the minimum set of IDs, for example, no need to keep all usage_item ID because probably they can be found using usage.id
Cursor: Proposed a minimal tracking approach using only `newsletterId`. Most resources can be found via `newsletterId`: subscribers delete by `newsletterId`, audience clusters found via join table, usage records delete by identifier, and newsletter chats found via subscribers. Provided a cleanup helper function that deletes subscribers (cascades to newsletter chats/messages), audience clusters, and usage records (cascades to usage items).
---
Me: in the proposed implementation I don’t see how you would delete chats and newsletter messages and newsletter chats
Cursor: Updated the cleanup function to explicitly delete newsletter chats and messages. The function finds subscribers by `newsletterId`, finds newsletter chats by `subscriberId`, deletes newsletter messages first, then deletes newsletter chats, then deletes subscribers, audience clusters, and usage records.
---
Me: wait wait, I didn’t know cascade exist. if that exists, why be explicit?
Cursor: Confirmed that cascade deletes are configured: `newsletterChat.subscriberId` → `subscribers.id`, `newsletterMessages.newsletterChatId` → `newsletterChat.id`, and `usageItem.usageId` → `usageTable.id`. Simplified the cleanup function to rely on cascade deletes - only deleting subscribers (which cascades to newsletter chats/messages) and usage records (which cascades to usage items). Only audience clusters need explicit deletion since there’s no cascade configured.
---
** HERE I SWITCHED TO AGENT MODE**
Me: okay, how about implementing this, together with a simple integration test?
Cursor: Provided implementation code including the cleanup function, updated error handlers, and a comprehensive integration test that creates test data and verifies cleanup. The cleanup function deletes subscribers (cascades to newsletter chats/messages), audience clusters, and usage records (cascades to usage items), all within a transaction.
---
Letting the Cursor generate the solution after such a lengthy conversation gave me:
a solution that I actually understand
it works as I wanted it to work
it doesn’t change random parts of the code
Hope this was helpful to you, and next time you’ll ask a bunch of questions from your Agent, instead of trying to one-shot your next JIRA ticket. 😉
Now let’s see what other stuff you should keep an eye on this week:
📰 Weekly shoutout
📣 Share
There’s no easier way to help this newsletter grow than by sharing it with the world. If you liked it, found something helpful, or you know someone who knows someone to whom this could be helpful, share it:
🏆 Subscribe
Actually, there’s one easier thing you can do to grow and help grow: subscribe to this newsletter. I’ll keep putting in the work and distilling what I learn/learned as a software engineer/consultant. Simply sign up here:
